content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
ApriI l-2, 199o
Linda M. Tangren, Chair
NWPC Of CALIFORNIA
123 Rainbow Road
Grass Val-IeY r CA 95949
Your Request for Advice
our FiIe No. A-9o-129
Dear l1s. Tangren:
You have requested advice on behalf of the National women's
Political- caucus of California (trthe caucusrr) concerning the
;;;;i;; 
provisions of the Politicat Reform Act (the I'Actrr) '1l 
on
rli.tn iq , 1990, r telephoned you for additional infornation
;;;;;.;i;q 
the first girestion-asked in vour retter of Februarv 12'
1990. You rephrased the question as foll-ows:
OUESTIONS
1. 
May the caucus transfer or loan funds frorn its general
op"..ii.rg ac-count to its sponsored committee's carnpaign account?
2. 
Are there any provisions requiring the campaign account
to have a separate set of bYlaws?
CONCLUSTONS
1. 
The caucus rnay transfer or loan funds frorn its genera)-
operating account to its campaign account as long as the amount
uling roined or transferred does not exceed the contribution
Iimiiations irnposed by the Act. 
You may want to.contact the
Franchise Tax board oi tne fnternal Revenue Service to determine
;;a 
ah" tax inplications may be in connection with the naking of
such loans or transfers.
2. 
The Act does not contain any provisions which require an
organization or its canpaign commj-ttee to establish bylaws'
1/ covernment code Sections 8I000-9Io15. A11 statutory references
are to the Governnent code unless otherwise indicated. 
Commission
regulations appear at 2 california Code of Regulations . Section
18ooo, et seq. A11 references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suitc 8OO o P.O. Box 8O7 o S:rcranrcnro CA 95tlLr.+-Lr8Q7 . (916) JZZ-566O


Linda M. Tangren
Page 2
FACTS
The National Women's Political caucus of California was
formed in February 1971- The Caucus has three separate bank
accounts: an operlting expense fund (the irgeneral fundfi), an
education fund-, and a-fund for making contributions to candidates
(the ''campaign accountrr). During our telephone conversation of
iaicn r+,'yori indicated that the general fund is conprised of a
;;;ai"; 
or'tn" membership dues paid- to the national association'
i"" if=" indicated that the Caucus has not made contributions or
loans to its carnpaign account frour the qeneral fund in the past
ind that the campaign account nornally receives its rnoney from
direct fundraising.
ANALYSI S
The Act inposes limitations on contributions made to
po:-iticif 
comrniltees and broad based political 
conrnittees within a
;i;;;i-t4". 
(Juty 1-June 30).2/ I'Political committeesr and "broad
f"r"a piriitiiaf 
Lomrnitteestt nay receive- up to $2,5o0 qer fiscal'
vear from a person. (Section ASfOZ') rrPersonrr is defined as an
inJiviauar, iroprietorship, 
f irrn, partnership, joint venture,
=v"ai"ii", 
' uirs iness trust, company, corporation, association'
c'ommittee, and labor organization. (Section 85102(b) ')
;p"liti"ui 
cornmittee,' ii a connittee of persons which receives
contributions from two or nore Persons and acting in concert makes
contributions to candidates. (section 85102(c).) "Broad based
p"iiii""f 
committeerr is a courmittee of persons which has been in
lxistence for more than six nonths, receives contributions from
one hundred or more persons, and acting in concert nakes
contributions to five or more candidates. (Section 85102(d) ')
Since there has not been a history of rnaking contributions
from the general fund, the caucus is lirnited to transferrj'nq not
more than $2,500 per fiscal year to the canpaign account.
(Section 85302.) Once the caucus has established a history of
using a portion of the mernbership dues for political 
purposes, the
rnemb6rs lre deerned to be on notice that a portion of their dues
may, in the future, be used for political purposes. Thereafter,
the portion of dues used to make contributions will be considered
contributions from each rnember and not the caucus.3/
2/ Trre term rtcontribution'r in the Act includes a loan. 
(Section
82015. )
3/ Generally, the Commission has advised that an organization
which begins naking contributions from funds such as rnernbership
dues is not rrreceiving" contributions from its members for one
calendar year (January l-December 31). That year,s activity
establishes a rrhistoryrr of naking contributions and wilI put the
members on notice that a portion of their dues may be used for
political 
purposes .


Linda M. Tangren
Page 3
An organization which uses only a portion of the money it
receives (in the form of menbership dues) for making contributions
or expenditures to support or oppose candidates or ballot neasures
is required to report as "contributions received" only that
portion of its receipts which is actually spent in connection with
supporting or opposing candidates or measures. In order to ensure
that the contributions received by the campaign account do not
exceed the linitations 
imposed by the Act, you must combine each
member's direct contributions to the campaign account vrith the
allocab1e portion of her dues which are transferred from the
general fund to deternine hor{ much the canpaign account has
received from each contributor.
section 84211(f) requires that a1l. contributions received
fron a single source aggregating $100 or more during a calendar
year be iternized on the comnittee's carnpaign statement (Forn 420).
The foLlowing example illustrates the rnethod for determining what
portion of each member's dues nust be counted as a contrj,bution:
ft tne Caucus receives a total of $I00,000 in membership dues and
transfers $1o,ooo of those dues to the campaign account, 10
percent of the funds received by the caucus are [contributionsrr
within the rneaninq of the Act. Thus, 10 percent of the total
amount of each nember's dues counts toward deternining whether
that particular member has nade a contribution of $100 or more to
the caropaign account. If the allocable portion of a memberts
dues, lrhen combined with his or her direct contributions to the
campaign account, totaL $100 or more during a calendar year, that
member,s name, address, occupation and employer must be itenized
on the recipient conmittee canpaign statement (Form 420). In
addition, each member nay contribute to the canpaign account no
more than 92,500 per fiscal year in the forrn of direct
contributions and dues transferred from the general funds.
(Section 85302. )
we are unable to answer your question concerning the legal
requirements for establishing a separate set of corporate bylaws
for the canpaign account. The Act does not address this issue.
I hope the information provided above is helpful. 
Please
contact me at 916/322-5662 if you have additional questions
concerning this natter.
S incere Iy I
Kathryn E.
Donovan
Genera I
「η
BY:
λκ
胎聖難し
Pol
ical Reforn Consultant
